---
title: "Planning as a Control Layer"
chapter_id: "planning-as-a-control-layer"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-27"
primary_sources:
  - "planforge"
  - "viea"
  - "cognitive_compilation"
  - "software_magic_grimoire"
  - "moecot"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Four local raw-cache mappings are passage-reviewed; `moecot` remains connector/source-note mapped, and support remains argument until planner harnesses, dependency checks, context-demand tests, or runtime replanning traces exist."
  - "The plan_graph fixture validates schema/example consistency only; decomposition, dependency ordering, context-demand, and runtime replanning tests remain unimplemented."
  - "The implemented Planning Lean predicates cover finite plan-node records only; they do not prove planner quality, graph completeness, dependency soundness, or deployed replanning behavior."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `planning-as-a-control-layer` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-27 |
| Primary source records | `planforge`, `viea`, `cognitive_compilation`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `moecot` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `planforge`, `viea`; supporting: `cognitive_compilation`, `software_magic_grimoire`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `planforge`, `viea`, `cognitive_compilation`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `moecot`; passage-reviewed local raw cache: `planforge`, `viea`, `cognitive_compilation`, `software_magic_grimoire`; connector/source-note only: `moecot` |
| Test state | `plan_graph.valid.json` passes protocol fixture validation, and `AsiStackProofs.Planning` builds locally; decomposition, dependency ordering, context-demand, and runtime replanning tests remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter treats planning as a bounded control artifact. The repo validates a draft plan-graph fixture, but it does not yet validate plan quality, dependency soundness, authority preservation under replanning, or successful execution.

It follows command contracts because explicit commands are still inert until the stack decides what work should exist, what order it belongs in, and which boundaries must be consulted before dispatch.

Planning is therefore the stack's first refusal-friendly layer after command intake. A plan can expose a missing dependency, overbroad tool request, impossible budget, or violated stop condition before the system spends authority. That makes plans useful even when they do not execute.

::: {.asi-human-only}
## Human Reading Path

Once a command is structured, the stack still has to decide what work should exist. Planning is the layer that turns intent into ordered obligations: dependencies, budgets, risk limits, context needs, tool choices, review points, and stop conditions.

This chapter treats planning as control, not brainstorming. A plan is valuable only if downstream layers can inspect it, reject unsafe steps, dispatch bounded jobs, and preserve residual work instead of letting the planner's confidence become execution authority.
:::

## Problem

Goals need to become governed plans with dependencies, budgets, risk limits, tool choices, and stopping conditions.

Planning begins after the command contract is explicit. Its job is to decide what work should exist, in what order, with what context, tools, budgets, authority, risk controls, and verification. It should not own memory, perform side effects, or decide that safety constraints are inconvenient. PlanForge supplies the planning middleware frame; Cognitive Compilation supplies the compiler pipeline frame; VIEA supplies the execution spine that the plan must feed.

The planner is therefore a control layer, not a general cognition blob. It turns contracts into dispatchable structure while preserving constraints and leaving execution authority to downstream gates.

The planner's job is not to be clever at any cost. Its job is to keep the action surface legible: what is being attempted, why this order, what context is needed, which tools are requested, what authority is missing, how success will be checked, and where the work must stop.

The planner should therefore be judged by the quality of its refusals as much as by the elegance of its decomposition. A plan that blocks a node because context is missing, authority is inferred, or verification is unavailable is doing useful control work. A plan that dispatches everything because the graph looks plausible is not a control layer.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Planning cannot be collapsed into memory, reasoning, or execution because each layer has different authority and failure modes.

A memory layer retrieves context; it should not silently create work. A reasoning layer evaluates claims; it should not silently spend tool authority. An execution layer runs jobs; it should not be the first place dependencies and stop conditions are discovered. When these roles collapse, scope creep looks like initiative, tool overreach looks like competence, and replanning after failure becomes ad hoc.

Planning also needs to be separate from final answers because plans are cheap to reject. A bad plan can be repaired before side effects occur. A bad execution may need rollback, audit, or incident handling.

Collapsing planning into execution also hides opportunity for review. A human, tribunal, router, or verifier can inspect a plan graph before a tool call exists. Once the tool call runs, the system has moved from deliberation into effects.

This is why planning needs dispatch states. A plan node can be valuable as an idea, dependency, question, or risk note without being ready to become a job. If every node is treated as executable, the graph becomes a work generator instead of a boundary.

## Core Claim

[planning-as-a-control-layer.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Planning is a separate control layer that converts accepted goals into governed action without owning memory, reasoning, or side effects.

The claim remains at `argument` support. PlanForge supplies decomposition, dependency, tier, and scheduler vocabulary; VIEA supplies the accepted-command-to-artifact spine; Cognitive Compilation supplies compiler-pass and IR pressure; Software Magic Grimoire supplies bounded workflow language; MoECOT remains an implementation-reference route rather than local evidence for this chapter.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C maps this planning-control claim to all assigned source notes. Four local raw-cache mappings are now passage-reviewed. The mappings support planning as a bounded control artifact between accepted goals and execution, not planner quality, dependency soundness, context-demand prediction, or deployed replanning behavior. `moecot` remains connector/source-note mapped until usable raw text, code, logs, release artifacts, or benchmark records are imported or inspected.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `planforge` | Passage-reviewed: planning middleware decouples planning from execution through hierarchical decomposition, primitive schemas, plan optimization, dependency inference, scheduling, MVI tiering, fallback, and failure-driven replanning. | Conceptual whitepaper only; no verified scheduler, acyclicity checker, or benchmark result is present. |
| `viea` | Passage-reviewed: accepted commands feed an artifact spine with orchestration, routing, workflow-to-tool compilation, verification, runtime adapters, feedback, residuals, tools, benchmarks, and regression coverage. | Does not prove a deployed planner, runtime handoff, or end-to-end execution trace. |
| `cognitive_compilation` | Passage-reviewed: plan formation, semantic compilation, target compilation, execution, and repair are separated while goals and constraints become source plans, semantic atoms, DAGs, validators, and repairable artifacts. | No local compiler, planner harness, task trace suite, or empirical ablation validates planning quality. |
| `software_magic_grimoire` | Passage-reviewed: workflows can be represented as spell stacks with handoffs, guards, entry/exit rules, loops, recursion, recovery paths, evidence-advancing loops, and mode separation. | Operational vocabulary only; no workflow engine, prompt-stack benchmark, or behavioral planning test is present. |
| `moecot` | Connector/source-note mapped: runtime-reference context for compact orchestration, specialist lanes, fail-closed ledgers, readiness gates, replay, residual tracking, promotion blockers, and handoff. | Runtime and benchmark claims remain source-reported until artifacts are inspected or reproduced. |

## Mechanism

Once the command fields exist, planning turns them into controllable work. PlanForge supplies decomposition, dependency, scheduling, tier, fallback, and replanning vocabulary. Cognitive Compilation supplies compiler-pass pressure, VIEA supplies the accepted-command-to-artifact spine, Software Magic Grimoire supplies guarded workflow language, and MoECOT remains a runtime-reference route for ledgers, readiness gates, replay, and residuals.

A Plan Graph is the bounded control artifact that captures this work without granting the planner memory ownership, tool authority, or permission to rewrite the governing contract.

The reviewed passages support the shape of that artifact: PlanForge gives the planner/executor split and DAG scheduling vocabulary; VIEA gives the artifact, router, verification, runtime, and feedback spine; Cognitive Compilation gives source-plan, semantic-DAG, scheduler, validator, and localized-repair vocabulary; Software Magic Grimoire gives handoffs, guards, exit rules, loop discipline, recursion limits, and recovery paths. None of the reviewed passages show this repository executing a planner, proving dependency soundness, predicting context demand, or replaying a runtime replanning trace.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Command contract"] --> B["Assumption extraction"]
  B --> C["Task graph"]
  C --> D["Dependency + context requests"]
  D --> E["Authority / risk / budget annotation"]
  E --> F["Verification plan"]
  F --> G{"Dispatchable?"}
  G -- "yes" --> H["Typed jobs"]
  G -- "no" --> I["Plan residual / replan"]
  H --> J["Runtime feedback"]
  J --> K{"Replan needed?"}
  K -- "yes" --> C
  K -- "no" --> L["Plan ledger"]
  I --> L
```

The control layer compiles accepted work into strategic, tactical, and runtime plans. Strategic planning chooses the broad route. Tactical planning decomposes work and dependencies. Runtime planning creates dispatchable nodes, context requests, authority requirements, budgets, verification plans, replanning policies, stop conditions, and failure behavior. The boundaries matter because execution still happens only through typed contracts downstream, and only authorized runtime layers should request specific tools, adapters, or human approvals.

The planner should be allowed to say "I do not have enough context," "this dependency is cyclic," "this tool exceeds authority," or "this budget cannot satisfy the quality predicate." Those outputs are useful plan residuals, not failures to be hidden.

Planning should preserve alternatives without pretending all alternatives are approved. A plan may list candidate routes, but only dispatchable nodes with satisfied constraints, authority, context, and verification plans should lower into typed jobs. The rest remain options, residuals, or review items.

Plan nodes should carry lifecycle states:

| State | Meaning | Lowering rule |
|---|---|---|
| `proposed` | A node is useful for decomposition but not yet checked. | No job creation. |
| `blocked_context` | Required context or source adequacy is missing. | Request context or split task. |
| `blocked_authority` | Required means, tool, publication, or effect lacks authority. | Re-contract or approval route. |
| `blocked_dependency` | Upstream node, artifact, or review is incomplete. | Wait or replan. |
| `blocked_verification` | Success predicate or verifier is missing. | Define verifier or narrow output. |
| `dispatchable` | Constraints, context, authority, dependencies, and verification plan are satisfied. | May lower into a typed job. |
| `dispatched` | The node has produced a job packet. | Track job parentage. |
| `replanned` | The node changed after feedback while preserving contract boundaries. | Record delta and residuals. |
| `stopped` | Stop condition, denial, budget exhaustion, or risk gate ended the node. | Preserve reason and evidence. |

These states let the plan graph be useful before execution and accountable after execution. They also keep candidate routes from being confused with authorized work.

## Interfaces

The interface is the Plan Graph.

- Alignment filters goals.
- VCM supplies context packets.
- Routing selects specialists.
- Execution runs typed jobs.

Minimum fields:

- `plan_id`
- `command_contract`
- `assumptions`
- `nodes`
- `dependencies`
- `context_requests`
- `tool_requirements`
- `authority_requirements`
- `authority_budget`
- `risk_budget`
- `compute_budget`
- `verification_plan`
- `replanning_policy`
- `replanning_history`
- `stop_conditions`
- `node_lifecycle_states`
- `blocked_nodes`
- `dispatch_receipts`
- `residual_register`
- `dispatch_state`
- `non_claims`

Alignment filters goals before planning. VCM supplies context packets. Routing consumes capability requirements. Execution receives typed jobs. Evidence records the plan's assumptions, residuals, verification results, and replanning events.

The plan graph is also the handoff between cognition and scheduling. It should say which nodes are exploratory, which are executable, which require approval, which are blocked, and which are evidence-only. Without that state, downstream execution has to infer whether a node is a thought, a task, a request, or a permission grant.

The public schema now forces the graph to name authority budget, replanning history, node lifecycle states, blocked nodes, dispatch receipts, residual register, and non-claims. Those fields do not prove decomposition quality or dependency correctness. They make the plan's dispatch boundary reviewable and give later tests a stable target.

A dispatch receipt should be produced when a node lowers into a job:

- `plan_node_id`
- `parent_contract_id`
- `dispatch_state`
- `satisfied_constraints`
- `context_packet_refs`
- `authority_basis`
- `verification_plan_ref`
- `replanning_history`
- `typed_job_ref`

This receipt is the narrow bridge to the Labor OS. If it is missing, execution can still be attempted by an ad hoc tool caller, but the book should not treat that as governed job dispatch.

## Invariants

- Plans expose constraints and stop conditions.
- Runtime replanning preserves authority limits.
- Tool selection is justified by task requirements.
- Only dispatchable nodes lower into typed jobs.
- Candidate routes, blocked nodes, and review notes are not executable permission.

A plan node inherits the parent command's authority ceiling unless governance narrows or explicitly changes it. Runtime replanning may change order, route, or decomposition, but it cannot broaden the authority boundary by calling itself adaptation.

Stop-condition preservation is equally important. Replanning after failure may find a new path, but it should not erase the condition that told the original plan when to stop, escalate, or ask for review.

## Failure modes

- Scope creep.
- Planning without replanning.
- Tool choice exceeds authority or budget.
- Dispatch laundering, where a proposed or blocked node becomes a job because it appears in the graph.
- Replanning erasure, where feedback changes the plan while hiding the authority, stop-condition, or residual delta.

Scope creep occurs when a plan adds goals that were never accepted. Planning without replanning means failures create improvisation instead of controlled repair. Tool choice exceeding authority or budget means the plan is not dispatchable, even if the work would be useful.

Another failure is plan theater: a graph that looks organized but does not control dispatch. If dependencies, authority, context demand, and verification are not checked before jobs are created, the graph is documentation rather than a control layer.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

A plan graph format with assumptions, task graph, dependencies, context and tool requirements, authority requirements, authority budget, risk and compute budgets, verification plan, replanning policy, replanning history, stop conditions, node lifecycle states, blocked-node records, dispatch receipts, residual register, non-claims, and failure behavior.

The smallest useful implementation is a `plan_graph` schema and fixture. It validates that a plan has nodes, dependencies, context and tool requirements, authority requirements, budgets, verification, replanning, stop conditions, dispatch receipts, residuals, non-claims, and dispatch state. It does not prove the plan is good.

The schema and fixture make the planning surface explicit. They do not prove that a graph is acyclic, optimal, complete, or safe to dispatch.

The next useful implementation step is a tiny plan-dispatch fixture with one valid node, one blocked dependency, one missing-context node, one authority-exceeding tool request, and one replanning event that preserves the original stop condition. That would demonstrate control-layer semantics without claiming planner quality.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature version is a live control plane for work, not a better brainstorming step. It receives accepted command contracts, builds and maintains plan graphs, and decides which nodes are merely proposed, blocked, reviewable, dispatchable, dispatched, replanned, or stopped. It never owns memory, authority, verification, or side effects; it coordinates the handoffs that let those layers do their own jobs.

As a control layer, planning is useful even when nothing executes. The planner can expose that a request is underspecified, a dependency is cyclic, a source is inadequate, a verifier is missing, a tool exceeds authority, a budget cannot support the quality predicate, or a stop condition has fired. Those outputs become plan residuals, not hidden failures.

The final product surface would include:

- Strategic, tactical, and runtime plan views over the same parent contract, each with assumptions, dependencies, context requests, budgets, risk limits, verification plans, and stop conditions.
- Node lifecycle states that prevent proposed, blocked, exploratory, or review-only nodes from lowering into jobs.
- Dispatch receipts that bind every job to a plan node, satisfied constraints, authority basis, context packet, verifier, and parent command.
- Replanning deltas that record what changed after feedback, which obligations were preserved, and which residuals or stop conditions remain.
- Control dashboards that show blocked context, blocked authority, blocked verifier, budget pressure, dependency pressure, and human approval queues before side effects occur.

This traffic-control layer remains a target architecture, not a current-result claim. Support should stay at `argument` until planner schemas, lifecycle enforcement, dispatch tests, replanning traces, and authority-preservation checks show that candidate work, authorized work, blocked work, and executed work remain separate under pressure.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Plan graph fixture validation | Check that the plan-graph fixture matches the public schema and declares assumptions, nodes, dependencies, context, tools, authority budget, risk and compute budgets, verification, replanning history, stop conditions, lifecycle states, dispatch receipts, residuals, non-claims, and dispatch state. | implemented by protocol validation; run in this pass |
| Authority inheritance finite-record proof | Check that a modeled plan node inherits the parent contract authority ceiling unless governance lowers it. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.Planning`; runtime replanning test not run |
| Unsatisfied-constraint finite-record proof | Check that a modeled plan node with unsatisfied required constraints cannot dispatch. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.Planning`; planner harness not run |
| Decomposition accuracy test | Check whether plan nodes preserve the accepted command objective and constraints. | planned; not run |
| Dependency ordering test | Check whether dependencies precede dependents before dispatch. | planned; not run |
| Context-demand prediction test | Check whether each node declares needed context before execution. | planned; not run |
| Runtime replanning test | Check whether replanning preserves authority, stop conditions, and residual history. | planned; not run |
| Dispatch-state enforcement test | Check that only `dispatchable` nodes can lower into typed jobs and blocked/proposed nodes remain residuals or review items. | planned; not run |
| Replanning-delta audit | Check that replanning records changed assumptions, authority basis, context, verification, stop conditions, and residuals. | planned; not run |

Only the fixture-shape check is implemented. The planned tests require a planner harness, dependency checker, context-demand oracle, or replay trace before the support state can move.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:planning.control_layer.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.Planning` | A plan node inherits the authority ceiling of its parent contract unless governance lowers it. | implemented |
| `lean:planning.control_layer.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.Planning` | A plan with unsatisfied required constraints cannot be dispatched. | implemented |

These proof hooks are implemented only over finite Lean plan-node records in `AsiStackProofs.Planning`. They prove that a modeled node inherits the parent authority ceiling when no governance lowering is present, and that a modeled node with unsatisfied required constraints is not dispatchable. They do not prove plan quality, acyclicity, dependency ordering, context-demand prediction, route choice, or deployed replanning behavior.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `planforge` | PlanForge | planning_control | Planning substrate. Goal-to-execution compilation, hierarchical decomposition, DAG planning, scheduling, intelligence arbitrage. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `cognitive_compilation` | Cognitive Compilation | planning_semantic_ir | Compiler framing for LLM-centered planning, semantic IR, target compilation, incremental repair. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `software_magic_grimoire` | Software Magic Grimoire | command_contracts_promptcraft | Structured command contracts and semantic density of software words. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `moecot` | MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper | implementation_reference | Concrete implementation evidence: governed low-parameter multi-core runtime, readiness gates, ledgers, replay. | source note available; connector or recovery required |

The planning/control boundary is supported by four passage-reviewed mappings, while `moecot` remains unreviewed. The core claim stays at `argument` because no planner harness, dependency checker, context-demand test, runtime replanning trace, or reproduced benchmark is recorded.

## Summary

Planning as a Control Layer converts accepted commands into governed structure. It exposes assumptions, tasks, dependencies, context requests, tools, authority, risk, budgets, verification, stop conditions, and replanning policy before execution begins.

This layer makes action governable by making non-action explicit: missing context, blocked authority, cyclic dependencies, exhausted budget, and failed predicates all become plan residuals instead of hidden improvisation. The next chapter specializes that graph into a schedulable DAG so efficiency claims have to name capability tiers, verification burdens, and residual costs.

The later typed-job chapter depends on this boundary. A job should be born from a dispatchable plan node, not from a planner's enthusiasm. That keeps execution downstream of constraints rather than upstream of explanation.
